kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Fri 17-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall) remains the latest published print: cash $3,154.00, unchanged from yesterday's report because the LME is closed Sat/Sun and no Monday ring has occurred yet (this report refreshed Sun 19-Jul, ~06:00 London). The number is the same; the world around it is not — Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman) over the weekend and the US carried out its 7th consecutive night of strikes. ~18.2% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 17-Jul official $3,146.50 — still the latest published print (weekend, no new session). Cash–3M spread holds at +$7.50 backwardation, unchanged since Friday's close. The open question for Monday's ring: does the weekend's sharp re-escalation (Iran hitting five Gulf states, Kuwait's energy sector directly struck) pull the front end tighter still, or does the market fade Friday's positioning into the open. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382); $3,061.5 the structural pivot.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 17-Jul official 280,100 t — latest published (unchanged, weekend). The unbroken drawdown since early April (509,250 t on 02-Jan → 280,100, −45.0% YTD) leaves the thinnest visible cover of the cycle heading into a weekend of sharply escalating Gulf hostilities. Falling stocks + steady backwardation are the physical tightening signal; Monday's ring is the first chance to reprice the escalation.

kpi_cards.label: BRENT ($/bbl)
kpi_cards.delta: Brent rose to $88.09 Fri (+4.58%), up more than 14% on the week, as the ceasefire effectively collapsed and hostilities widened. Over the weekend Iran struck Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan and Oman — including a direct hit on a Kuwaiti oil-sector site — while US CENTCOM logged its 7th consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Energy is the largest smelter cost line; this is a re-escalation, not the de-escalation this dashboard flagged Saturday.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Fri 17-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall) $3,154.00 vs 16-Jul $3,170.00 = −$16.00 (−0.50%) — the latest published print; unchanged since Friday because the LME is closed over the weekend (report refreshed Sun 19-Jul). avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 17-Jul official $3,146.50 vs 16-Jul $3,166.50 (−$20.00, −0.63%) — latest published, unchanged over the weekend. Resistance stack: $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382 of the June–July decline). avg = June-2026 3M mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: BACKWARDATION holds at +$7.50 (cash $3,154.00 over 3M $3,146.50), unchanged since Friday's close — no new session over the weekend to test it. The backdrop has moved sharply, however: Iran struck five Gulf states over the weekend and the US logged a 7th consecutive night of strikes, re-escalating exactly the disruption this spread has been pricing. Monday's ring is the first read on whether the front end tightens further.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (S&P Global Platts, assessed 06-Jul) — a decade high (+12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353). SMM flags spot tightness and squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against the decade-high anchor; the swing variable is whether the Hormuz disruption persists or the US–Iran diplomacy (MoU to ~18-Aug) restores flow. Source: Platts via IndexBox. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: Spot firming toward the Q3 settlement: Platts CIF Japan spot last publicly at ~$370/t (06-Jul); SMM's 'squeeze risk climbs under LME deep backwardation' analysis flags tight spot supply pushing the QMJP premium up. Point ~$365/t. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343 / SMM weekly.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts P1020A duty-UNPAID in-whs Rotterdam last publicly assessed ~$525–560/t (mid $542.50, 03-Jul) — no fresher public print. Structural floor: 6% EU duty + CBAM + Mozal (~580 ktpa) on care & maintenance. Gulf war-risk freight is the live swing leg; the toll threat died 14-Jul and diplomacy is now easing the tail-risk (S&P Global / SMM).

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs duty-paid Rotterdam — last public reference (~late-Jun; no fresh public print). Conversion premia are stickier than ingot premia; Mozal remains down. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last publicly assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul). The all-in US delivered price embeds the 50% Section-232 tariff (≈$1,577/t at the 17-Jul cash of $3,154), so the policy leg dominates. Alcoa's Q2 flagged Section-232 on Canadian metal rising ~$35m q/q. Verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: SMM weekly DDP print 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul). Duty-paid = LME + Section-232 tariff (50%) + market premium; the policy leg is ~two-thirds of the all-in. The tariff-$ component eased ~$8/t with Friday's softer LME. Verify Platts/Fastmarkets.

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Rose to $88.09 Fri (+4.58%), up more than 14% on the week — the sharpest weekly gain since the conflict's earlier escalations. The mid-June Islamabad-brokered ceasefire has effectively collapsed since ~08-Jul; over the weekend Iran struck Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan and Oman while the US carried out a 7th consecutive night of strikes on Iranian targets. Energy is the largest smelter cost line.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY ~100.7, little changed from Friday after last week's soft-inflation prints (CPI −0.4% 14-Jul, PPI −0.3% 15-Jul). July Fed-hike odds ~20%, September ~60% (swaps). A softer dollar removes a headwind for metals even as the geopolitical risk premium re-widens.

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Euro ~1.147, steady. The ECB still carries a war-energy inflation problem with Brent now near $88 and European gas prices exposed to any further Gulf escalation.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.77. June data stayed aluminium-bearish: RECORD unwrought-aluminium exports of 711 kt (+12.5% m/m; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y — GACC) against Q2 GDP of 4.3% y/y (slowest since 2022) — production strong, domestic demand weak, exports the release valve even as the Gulf side of the market tightens.

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen ~162.4, little changed. A still-weak yen cushions Japan-bound cargo premia in local terms; MJP Q4 talks (~Sep) now open against a decade-high $395 anchor AND a re-escalating Gulf conflict rather than the fading disruption assumed as recently as Saturday.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: 10Y ~4.56% after two cool inflation prints (CPI −0.4%, PPI −0.3%) pulled July hike odds to ~20%. Real rates remain the macro cap on metals; the swing risk this week is geopolitical (oil-driven inflation pass-through), not the Fed.

macro_events: MATERIAL WEEKEND DEVELOPMENT — RE-ESCALATION, NOT DE-ESCALATION: the mid-June Islamabad-brokered ceasefire, already fraying since ~08-Jul, broke down further over the weekend. Iran struck FIVE Gulf states — Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan and Oman — in retaliation for renewed US strikes on Iranian cities; US CENTCOM logged its 7th consecutive night of strikes early Saturday, hitting surveillance sites, logistics infrastructure and maritime capabilities. This reverses the 'strikes easing / diplomacy advancing' read published Saturday.

KUWAIT ENERGY SECTOR DIRECTLY HIT: Kuwait Petroleum Corporation said Saturday a 'vital site in the oil sector' sustained 'repeated, brutal Iranian attacks' with material losses and injuries; Iran also struck a Kuwaiti desalination plant on both Friday and Saturday. Qatar's foreign ministry condemned the attacks on Jordan, Bahrain and Kuwait. Both Kuwait and Bahrain host US forces.

STRAIT TRAFFIC NEAR STANDSTILL: transits fell to as few as 8 vessels on Thursday 16-Jul (vs a normal ~60/day) per Kpler/MarineTraffic data cited by NPR; Lloyd's List and the Hormuz Strait Monitor report war-risk cover for Gulf transits has widened sharply (quoted around $10–14m per voyage on some routes, rates ~2.5%, up to ~5% for US/UK/Israel-linked vessels).

OIL SPIKED ON THE ESCALATION: Brent rose to $88.09 (+4.58% Friday), up >14% on the week — the direct read-through to smelter power costs. The original 60-day Islamabad MoU deadline lands around mid-to-late August, but Iran has signalled it now considers the US in violation and is suspending commitments under it (per live wire reporting) — the ~18-Aug date is a much weaker anchor for de-escalation than as of Friday.

ALCOA Q2 (reported 16-Jul, unchanged since) still frames the producer read: record $1.073bn Aluminum-segment EBITDA (32.3% margin) on near-decade-high $3.97bn revenue, but adj. EPS $2.12 missed the ~$2.31–2.32 consensus on higher costs including ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal; shares fell.

CHINA (unchanged since Wed): Q2 GDP +4.3% y/y (slowest since 2022) with June IP +5.3% but retail sales +1.0% — the production-heavy, demand-light mix that keeps the record 711 kt monthly export valve open (NBS/GACC), a supply-side offset to the Gulf-side tightening.
lme_commentary: The 17-Jul official is still the latest published LME print (cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50 / stock 280,100 t) because the exchange is closed Saturday and Sunday; this report was refreshed Sun 19-Jul ~06:00 London with no new session to test the tape. What has changed is the backdrop the tape must now open against: the Islamabad-brokered ceasefire, already fraying since ~08-Jul, broke down further over the weekend — Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman), including a direct hit on a Kuwaiti oil-sector site, and the US logged a 7th consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Strait transits are near a standstill (as few as 8 vessels Thursday vs a normal ~60/day) and Brent jumped to $88.09 (+4.58% Friday, >14% on the week). The cash–3M backwardation held at +$7.50 into the weekend on a 280,100 t stock base (−45.0% YTD) — the physical-tightness signal this dashboard has tracked for a week — but it has not yet had the chance to reprice a materially worse escalation. Technically, the market must hold the $3,138 shelf and reclaim $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382) to validate a catch-up to the backwardation; a break of $3,061.5 negates the recovery. Next LME ring: Monday 20-Jul — the first opportunity for the tape to reflect the weekend's news.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$338/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference; the surplus story is intact (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarted 10-Jul; oversupply persists). Delivered cargoes into the Gulf still carry war-risk cover, though the easing US–Iran conflict trims the tail. Verify SMM/Fastmarkets.

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$303/t — easing on the building surplus (Q1 avg $306.9; latest public reference). SMM flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June — a partial offset to the EGA restart. Verify Platts.

alumina.name: SHFE alumina futures (AO2609)
alumina.src: Last public futures print in the ¥2,700s (SMM); price below moving averages, port stocks building. SMM domestic spot weighted average ~¥2,550/t. The strait squeeze still weighs on Gulf refinery feed (~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz) even as the global balance stays long (SMM).

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$308/t — CME/TradingView; no material move in the latest public print.

alumina_notes: The alumina market remains the mirror image of metal: globally LOOSENING (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa restart, Guinea bauxite oversupply ~$68/dmt CIF China, Chinese port stocks building; domestic China spot ~¥2,550 and drifting) — ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band holds. No fresher public alumina print surfaced over the weekend (Fri close carries forward).

The regional wrinkle has widened, not eased: Iran's weekend strikes on Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan and Oman — including a direct hit on Kuwaiti energy infrastructure — re-escalate exactly the risk that prices war-risk cover into the ~60% of Gulf alumina imports that transit Hormuz. Strait transits are near a standstill (as few as 8 vessels Thursday vs a normal ~60/day). SMM separately flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June.

Guinea's pending export-quota policy (targeting >$100/dmt bauxite) remains the one clear bullish catalyst on the commodity leg; record Australian and Metro Mining shipments underline how long the seaborne market currently is.
net_read: STEADY TAPE, WORSENING BACKDROP. The 17-Jul official is unchanged from Friday because the LME is closed over the weekend — cash $3,154, 3M $3,146.50, cash–3M backwardation holding at +$7.50 on the thinnest stocks of the cycle (280,100 t, −45.0% YTD). What has moved is everything around the tape: the ceasefire has effectively broken down since ~08-Jul, and over the weekend Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman) — including a direct hit on Kuwaiti energy infrastructure — while the US logged a 7th consecutive night of strikes. Brent jumped to $88.09 (+4.58%, >14% on the week) and strait transits are near a standstill. This is a materially worse geopolitical starting point for Monday's ring than the 'strikes easing, diplomacy advancing' read this dashboard published Saturday. Net: the physical-squeeze case (backwardation, drawn stocks, Gulf disruption) has gained evidence over the weekend while the offsetting case (record Chinese exports, soft US inflation) is unchanged — the burden of proof has shifted toward the bull scenario into Monday's open, though nothing is confirmed until the tape actually trades. Alcoa's Q2 (record aluminium margins, EPS miss on tariff/cost drag) still frames the producer reality. Situational awareness, not advice.

feed.text: WEEKEND ESCALATION, NOT DE-ESCALATION: Iran struck five Gulf states — Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman — through Saturday into Sunday, in retaliation for renewed US strikes; Qatar's foreign ministry condemned the attacks. This reverses the 'flare-up easing' read published Saturday. LME closed; next official Mon 20-Jul (NPR / Al Jazeera / ABC News).

KUWAIT ENERGY SECTOR STRUCK: Kuwait Petroleum Corporation reported 'repeated, brutal Iranian attacks' on a 'vital site in the oil sector' with material losses and injuries; a Kuwaiti desalination plant was also hit on both Friday and Saturday. US CENTCOM logged its 7th consecutive night of strikes on Iranian surveillance, logistics and maritime targets (CBS/NPR/Fox News).

OIL SPIKES ON RE-ESCALATION: Brent rose to $88.09 (+4.58%), up more than 14% on the week — the fastest weekly gain since the conflict's earlier flashpoints. Strait of Hormuz transits fell to as few as 8 vessels Thursday (vs a normal ~60/day, Kpler/MarineTraffic via NPR); war-risk insurance quotes widened sharply (Lloyd's List / Hormuz Strait Monitor).

BACKWARDATION HOLDS: the 17-Jul official — still the latest published print over the weekend — left the cash–3M spread at +$7.50 on stock of 280,100 t (−45.0% YTD). The front end has been pricing Gulf tightness for a week; the weekend's escalation is the first real test of whether that pricing was conservative (Westmetall).

ALCOA Q2 MISS DESPITE RECORD MARGINS (unchanged since Thu): revenue $3.97bn (+24% q/q, best in nearly a decade) and a RECORD Aluminum-segment EBITDA of $1.073bn (32.3% margin), but adj. EPS $2.12 missed the ~$2.31 consensus on higher costs (incl. ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal); shares fell (BusinessWire / Investing.com).

CHINA Q2 GDP +4.3% y/y (unchanged since Wed) — slowest since 2022; June IP +5.3% beat, retail +1.0% soft. Production-heavy, demand-light: the mix behind the record 711 kt monthly export flow into the ex-China market (NBS via CNBC/FXStreet).

news.headline: WAR RE-ESCALATES SHARPLY OVER THE WEEKEND: the Islamabad-brokered ceasefire has effectively collapsed since ~08-Jul. Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman) Sat–Sun, hitting a Kuwaiti oil-sector site and a desalination plant; the US logged a 7th consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Strait transits near a standstill (as few as 8 vessels Thursday vs ~60/day normal).
news.source: NPR; Al Jazeera; ABC News; Fox News (17–19 Jul)

news.headline: TAPE UNCHANGED, BACKDROP WORSE: the 17-Jul official — cash $3,154.00, 3M $3,146.50, cash–3M +$7.50 backwardation — is still the latest published print because the LME is closed over the weekend. Monday's ring is the first chance to reprice the weekend's escalation against a 280,100 t stock base (−45.0% YTD).

OIL AND FREIGHT RISK REPRICE: Brent jumped to $88.09 (+4.58% Fri, >14% on the week); Gulf war-risk insurance quotes widened sharply (Lloyd's List / Hormuz Strait Monitor cite $10–14m per voyage on some routes). Baltic Dry fell to 2,752 (−3.1% d/d, >6% on the week) as dry-bulk demand softened even as the Gulf risk premium built elsewhere.
news.source: Trading Economics; Lloyd's List; Hormuz Strait Monitor; Hellenic Shipping News

news.headline: ALCOA Q2 MISS ON COSTS (unchanged since 16-Jul): revenue $3.97bn (best in ~a decade) and record Aluminum-segment EBITDA ($1.073bn, 32.3% margin), but adj. EPS $2.12 missed ~$2.31 on higher costs incl. ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal; shares fell.

RECORD EXPORTS, WEAK GROWTH (unchanged since Wed): China's June aluminium exports hit a record 711 kt (first >700 kt; H1 3.4 Mt, +16.3% y/y) while Q2 GDP slowed to 4.3%. The supply-side offset to Gulf-side tightening is unchanged, even as the geopolitical risk premium rebuilds.

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.97bn (+24% q/q, highest in nearly a decade); adj. EPS $2.12 (MISS vs ~$2.31 consensus); adj. net income $562m; adj. EBITDA $901m (from $595m in Q1); Aluminum segment RECORD EBITDA $1.073bn at 32.3% margin.
earnings.readthrough: First producer P&L under the Hormuz-disruption regime: record aluminium margins and near-decade-high revenue on higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums, but adj. EPS missed on higher costs — notably ~$35m more Section-232 tariff on Canadian metal into the US — and the shares fell. South32 upstream acquisition ($4.1bn upfront, up to $5.6bn contingent to 2030) progressing, close expected H1-2027. The tariff is a double edge: it inflates US premia but taxes Alcoa's cross-border Canadian metal.

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due Wed 22-Jul (07:00 CEST) — the next European producer read on power costs and premium realisation.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: H1 copper-equivalent output +3% y/y; aluminium division improved; strong bauxite recovery; FY aluminium guidance UNCHANGED at 3.25–3.45 Mt; Pilbara Q2 shipments 89 Mt (+5%).
earnings.readthrough: First upstream print of the Q2 season (this morning): Western volumes are intact — the Gulf disruption is expressing itself in prices, freight and premia rather than output. Kitimat/AP60 ramp continues; H1 financial results due 13-Aug.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings.headline: Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY.
earnings.readthrough: Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese producer margins.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each listed peer's most recent reported results as of 19-Jul (public, listed-company information) — unchanged since Alcoa reported 16-Jul (no new peer releases over the weekend). Q2 season is OPEN: Alcoa's record Aluminum-segment margins (EBITDA $1.073bn, 32.3%) and near-decade-high revenue ($3.97bn) came with an adj. EPS MISS ($2.12 vs ~$2.31 consensus) on higher costs including ~$35m more Section-232 tariff on Canadian metal; shares fell. Rio Tinto's production report (15-Jul) showed volumes intact and aluminium guidance unchanged (3.25–3.45 Mt). Next: Norsk Hydro Q2 Wed 22-Jul (07:00 CEST) — now the first European producer read to land after the weekend's re-escalation, making its commentary on Gulf-linked costs and premium realisation more consequential than it looked on Friday; Maaden (1211) H1 late-Jul/early-Aug (Tadawul). Context: Q2 average LME cash ≈ $3,459 (June mean) vs $3,154 at the latest official — sequentially strong realised prices with a softening tail heading into a materially more disrupted Q3.

commercial.text: 17-Jul official cash $3,154 is still the latest published print (LME closed over the weekend); the cash–3M backwardation holds at +$7.50 on −45.0% YTD stocks. The weekend brought a material escalation (Iran struck five Gulf states, Kuwait's energy sector hit directly, 7th night of US strikes) that the tape has not yet had a chance to price. Monday's ring — does it gap toward $3,230/$3,260 or fade — is the key near-term signal. Technical context, not advice.

MJP Q3 settled $395 (decade high, unchanged); CIF Japan spot ~$365 on squeeze risk under the deep backwardation. No fresher public premium print over the weekend. Given the re-escalation, Q4 MJP talks (~Sep) now open against both a decade-high anchor AND a materially worse Gulf security backdrop than assumed as of Friday — the case for a Q4 settlement toward the top of the $345–425 pending range has strengthened.

Alumina stays globally long on paper (EGA ramping, FOB Australia ~$338, API ~$303) but the weekend's direct strikes on Kuwaiti energy infrastructure and the near-standstill in strait transits raise the delivered-cost and reliability risk for any Gulf-routed alumina or bauxite cargo. Secondary feed stays the quiet tightener (UAE scrap export ban, EU tariff changes). Watch index-vs-delivered divergence closely into next week.

Re-set the cost stack to the RE-ESCALATION case, reversing Saturday's more benign framing: (1) war-risk quotes have widened sharply — Lloyd's List/Hormuz Strait Monitor cite $10–14m per voyage on some routes and rates as high as ~5% for US/UK/Israel-linked tonnage; (2) strait transits are near a standstill (as few as 8 vessels Thursday vs ~60/day normal); (3) the prior ~18-Aug MoU deadline is a much weaker anchor now that Iran has signalled it is suspending commitments under it. Keep force-majeure and war-risk clauses live and assume elevated freight/insurance costs persist into next week at minimum.

bottom_line: On 19-Jul the published tape is unchanged from Friday — the LME is closed over the weekend, so the 17-Jul official (cash $3,154, 3M $3,146.50, cash–3M +$7.50 backwardation, stock 280,100 t, −45.0% YTD) remains the latest print. What has changed sharply is the backdrop it will open against on Monday: the Islamabad-brokered ceasefire has effectively collapsed since ~08-Jul, and over the weekend Iran struck five Gulf states — Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan and Oman — including a direct hit on Kuwaiti energy infrastructure, while the US carried out a 7th consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Strait of Hormuz transits are near a standstill and Brent jumped to $88.09 (+4.58%, >14% on the week). This reverses the 'strikes easing, diplomacy advancing' read this dashboard published Saturday — the burden of proof has shifted back toward the physical-squeeze case (widening backwardation, thin stocks, an actively worsening Gulf conflict), though nothing is confirmed until Monday's ring actually trades. Alcoa's Q2 (record aluminium margins, EPS miss on tariff/cost drag) still frames the producer reality. The tell for the week ahead: does Monday's official gap toward $3,230/$3,260 on the escalation, or does the record Chinese export machine keep a lid on it even as the Gulf risk premium rebuilds?
sources: LME official settlements & stock (17-Jul, latest published — LME closed over the weekend)

London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com (Al cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50 / stock 280,100 t; Cu 3M $13,415.00 / Ni 3M $16,870.00 / Zn 3M $3,529.00 / Pb 3M $1,871.00, all 17-Jul officials confirmed. June monthly means from the same table.)
US-Iran war re-escalation over the weekend (17–19-Jul)

NPR — 'The U.S. and Iran blow past red lines as they lurch back toward all-out war' (18-Jul); Al Jazeera — 'Iran attacks five Gulf nations, shuts Hormuz after US bombing'; ABC News live updates — 'Iran says US violated MOU, says its suspending commitments'; Fox News live updates (18-Jul); Wikipedia '2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis' / '2026 Iran war'.
Kuwait energy-sector attack & Gulf strikes (18-Jul)
Kuwait Petroleum Corporation statement via wire reporting; Qatar Foreign Ministry statement condemning attacks on Jordan, Bahrain and Kuwait.
Strait transit collapse & war-risk insurance

Kpler/MarineTraffic data via NPR (transits down to ~8 vessels Thu 16-Jul vs normal ~60/day); Lloyd's List; Hormuz Strait Monitor (war-risk quotes $10–14m per voyage on some routes; rates to ~5% for US/UK/Israel-linked tonnage).
Alcoa Q2 2026 results (16-Jul, unchanged since)

Alcoa Corporation Q2 2026 release (news.alcoa.com; BusinessWire) — revenue $3.97bn, adj. EPS $2.12 (miss vs ~$2.31), adj. EBITDA $901m, Aluminum segment record EBITDA $1.073bn / 32.3% margin; Section-232 on Canadian metal +~$35m q/q.
US June PPI & CPI (14–15-Jul, unchanged since)
BLS — CPI −0.4% m/m (14-Jul) and PPI −0.3% m/m (15-Jul); CNBC. Swap-implied July hike odds ~20%.
China June exports (record) & Q2 GDP (unchanged since Wed)

caveats: Report refreshed Sun 19-Jul-2026, ~06:00 London — the LME is closed both Saturday and Sunday, so the latest PUBLISHED official remains Fri 17-Jul (cash $3,154.00 / 3M $3,146.50 / stock 280,100 t — Westmetall, confirmed unrevised). This is not a stale reuse of numbers — it is genuinely the latest published print; the next LME ring is Monday 20-Jul.

MATERIAL CAVEAT: the geopolitical backdrop moved sharply over the weekend and is NOT yet reflected in any LME print. Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman) between Friday night and Sunday morning, including a direct hit on Kuwaiti energy infrastructure; the US logged a 7th consecutive night of strikes. This reverses the 'strikes easing, diplomacy advancing' framing published in Saturday's report — treat Friday's flat-price close as pre-escalation and expect Monday's ring to be the first real test.

The base-metals board now carries confirmed 17-Jul LME officials for all five metals (aluminium, copper, nickel, zinc, lead) via Westmetall — an improvement on Saturday's report, which had to use 16-Jul data for copper/nickel/zinc/lead because the 17-Jul officials were not yet posted at that run's time. Day % is each metal's latest-session move; YTD is vs the 02-Jan-2026 3M.

Premiums, alumina and the overseas physical complex show the latest PUBLIC prints (MJP Q3 settlement 06-Jul; SMM/Platts marks early-to-mid Jul); no fresher public prints surfaced over the weekend. They lag the fast-moving Gulf logistics picture even in normal weeks — with the weekend's escalation, treat delivered-cost figures as more stale than usual until fresh weekday prints post. Subscription indices (Platts/Fastmarkets/SMM) should be verified for any transaction.

Everything published here is PUBLIC market information only — no confidential, internal or company-specific data. The only company-specific mentions are public listed-company facts in the peer-earnings table (Alcoa, Norsk Hydro, Century, Rio Tinto, Maaden (1211), etc.). Nothing here is trading, hedging or investment advice; Elliott Wave and technical notes are analytical context, not recommendations.

Geopolitical facts (Iran's strikes on five Gulf states, the Kuwaiti energy-sector attack, strait transit data, war-risk insurance levels) are compiled from public wire reporting (NPR/Al Jazeera/ABC News/Fox News/Lloyd's List/Hormuz Strait Monitor) that is evolving hour-by-hour over the weekend; figures were current at ~06:00 London on 19-Jul and may have moved materially by the time of reading.

forward_path.basis: Latest PUBLISHED official (Westmetall); −0.50% vs 16-Jul — net flat on the week after a $3,171 mid-week peak. The cash–3M spread widened to +$7.50 backwardation. Next official Mon 20-Jul.
Latest published official; −0.63% vs 16-Jul; front spread +$7.50 BACKWARDATION (from +$3.50). Resistance $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382).

outlook.curve_note: The diagnostic held steady into a weekend that moved sharply against it. The cash–3M spread was last published at +$7.50 BACKWARDATION on the 17-Jul official — unchanged since Friday because the LME is closed — on a 280,100 t stock base (−45.0% YTD). What has changed is the disruption the spread was pricing: over the weekend Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman), including a direct hit on Kuwaiti energy infrastructure, and the ceasefire that underpinned last week's 'diplomacy advancing' framing has effectively collapsed. The counterweight is still quantified at record scale — China exported 711 kt in June (first month ever >700 kt) into 4.3% GDP growth — but the swing factor has moved decisively toward renewed disruption rather than resolution. The 2026 consensus band ($3,200–3,500 averages) and the ~−365 kt structural deficit remain the floor beneath the argument; Monday's ring is the first chance for the curve to actually reprice the weekend's news.

scenarios.drivers: The weekend's re-escalation persists or worsens — the strait stays near a standstill, war-risk cover keeps widening, and the backwardation gaps wider on Monday's open as the market catches up to five Gulf states being struck
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the ceasefire has effectively collapsed and Iran is reported to be suspending commitments under the ~18-Aug MoU — the corridor mechanism that was meant to reopen the strait is no longer a credible near-term de-escalation path

Curve confirmation: Monday's official reopens the +$7.50 backwardation to price in the weekend's five-state strike campaign — the tight-prompt configuration (backwardation + stocks −45.0% YTD) gains fresh evidence rather than fading

Freight/insurance: war-risk quotes ($10–14m/voyage on some routes per Lloyd's List) and strait transits near a standstill (8 vessels Thursday vs ~60/day normal) directly raise delivered costs for any Gulf-linked aluminium, alumina or bauxite flow
Premiums: MJP Q4 talks (~Sep) now open against both the $395 decade-high anchor and a demonstrably worse security backdrop than assumed Friday
Targets: a Monday gap through $3,230/$3,260 opens $3,330–3,400 quickly; $3,600+ on confirmation the disruption is sustained (30-day+) rather than another flare-and-fade cycle

scenarios.drivers: The market treats the weekend as another flare-up in a pattern of strikes-then-talks that has repeated for months — front-end tightness persists but record Chinese exports and a still-open (if damaged) MoU framework cap a full re-rating
drivers_list: Geopolitics: despite the weekend's severity, the underlying Islamabad-MoU framework and international pressure (Qatar, Pakistan-brokered channels) eventually re-engage, echoing the pattern seen since April; sporadic risk keeps a front-of-curve premium without a sustained full closure
Supply: the ~−365 kt 2026 deficit persists but stays offset — record 711 kt June Chinese exports, EGA alumina ramping, Indonesia adding capacity, Rio volumes intact
China: Q2 GDP 4.3% with retail +1.0% — domestic softness keeps the export valve open; destocking supports the SHFE bottom but not a breakout
Macro: CPI/PPI relief holds but September rate-cut odds stay ~60% priced — DXY ~100–101, a modest tailwind that does not offset the geopolitical repricing
Premiums: MJP Q4 negotiates in the $360–420 band, biased toward the upper half of the prior $345–425 range given the weekend's events

scenarios.drivers: The escalation proves short-lived — a rapid diplomatic circuit-breaker (as seen multiple times already in this conflict) restores a functional corridor within days, unwinding the war premium into a market still carrying record Chinese supply
drivers_list: Geopolitics: a Qatar- or Pakistan-brokered de-escalation lands within days (this conflict has repeatedly flared and cooled since February) — a verified strait reopening or ceasefire restoration repeats the pattern of war-premium unwinds seen in April and June
Macro: a hot US data surprise re-prices September toward a done Fed deal — DXY back through 101.5 and metals de-rate as a complex, independent of the Gulf story

Supply: the record export machine (711 kt June, H1 +16.3%) keeps metal flowing into the off-season regardless of the Gulf outcome; EGA beats its ramp; premium weeklies print lower once the acute risk fades
Curve: the +$7.50 backwardation collapses back toward flat/contango once the market judges the escalation as another temporary flare rather than a structural regime change
Technical: a daily close below $3,061.5 re-opens $2,990–3,020, then the .618 at $2,894

risks.risk: Hormuz war RE-ESCALATED over the weekend: Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman), ceasefire effectively collapsed, strait transits near a standstill
Rapid de-escalation still possible: this conflict has repeatedly flared and cooled since February — a Qatar/Pakistan-brokered circuit-breaker could restore a functional corridor within days
Physical looseness at record scale: China June exports 711 kt (first >700 kt), Q2 GDP miss, EGA restart, off-season demand — unchanged and still the key supply-side offset
Front-end squeeze: cash–3M backwardation at +$7.50 on −45.0% YTD stocks, not yet repriced for the weekend's escalation — delivery/financing stress risk into Monday's ring
Gulf energy-infrastructure attacks widen: direct strikes on Kuwaiti oil and desalination assets raise the risk of collateral damage to regional smelter power and alumina refining capacity
US Section-232 at 50% distorts trade flows and premium realisation (tariff leg ≈$1,577/t; a ~$35m q/q cost drag on Alcoa's Canadian metal)

catalysts.event: Next LME ring — the first opportunity to reprice the weekend's five-state Gulf escalation; does the cash–3M backwardation gap wider and does the flat price test $3,230 (MA20)?
Further Iran/Gulf state strikes or a diplomatic circuit-breaker — either direction is now live and fast-moving after the weekend's re-escalation
Norsk Hydro Q2 (07:00 CEST) — European producer read on power costs, Extrusions demand and premium realisation, now more consequential given the weekend's Gulf energy-cost implications
The Islamabad MoU deadline nominally lands here, but Iran is reported to be suspending commitments under it — its relevance as a de-escalation anchor has weakened materially since Friday
Maaden (1211) H1 results (Tadawul) — Gulf producer read on realised prices and volumes through the renewed disruption
MJP Q4 settlement talks open against the $395 decade-high anchor — the pending-quarter range ($345–425) now biased toward the upper half given the weekend's escalation

ai_analysis: 19-Jul's defining feature is a gap between an unchanged tape and a materially worse backdrop: the LME is closed over the weekend so Friday's official (cash $3,154, cash–3M +$7.50 backwardation) is still the latest print, but Iran struck five Gulf states — Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman — between Friday night and Sunday morning, reversing the 'strikes easing, diplomacy advancing' framing this dashboard published Saturday.

The Kuwaiti energy-sector attack is the most commercially relevant single data point of the weekend: a direct strike on a 'vital site in the oil sector' plus a desalination plant signals the conflict is now touching regional energy and utility infrastructure adjacent to the Gulf's aluminium and alumina complex, not just shipping lanes.

The bear file keeps its best number of the year unchanged: 711 kt of Chinese exports in June — the first month ever above 700 kt — into an economy growing 4.3%, the slowest since 2022. That supply-side offset has not moved; what has moved is the Gulf-side tightening case, which gained fresh evidence over the weekend rather than the fading this dashboard flagged as possible on Saturday.

Alcoa's Q2 still grounds the producer reality: record aluminium-segment margins (32.3%) and near-decade-high revenue, but an EPS miss on higher costs — including ~$35m more Section-232 on Canadian metal. With Gulf freight and insurance costs now rebuilding rather than fading, the next quarter's cost commentary (starting with Norsk Hydro on 22-Jul) carries more weight than it did a week ago.

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled at $395/t CIF Japan (Platts, 06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM flags spot tightness and squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation, firming CIF spot toward ~$365. Q4 talks (~Sep) open against a decade-high anchor and a still-disrupted strait — with US–Iran diplomacy (MoU to ~18-Aug) the swing factor.

Platts duty-unpaid last publicly marked ~$525–560 (mid $542.50, 03-Jul). The 6% EU duty, CBAM and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor; the war-risk freight component is easing at the margin as the US–Iran conflict de-escalates. The prospective 20% strait toll died on 14-Jul.

SMM's weekly DDP print ~109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,577 at the 17-Jul LME cash) dominates the all-in — US premia track Washington and the LME, not local tightness. Alcoa Q2 confirmed the tariff as a ~$35m q/q cost on its Canadian metal.

The decomposition below separates the mechanical tariff leg (Section-232 rate × LME) from the residual market premium. Most of the US 'premium' is a policy transfer, not a scarcity signal — and the new 20% strait toll extends the same logic to the freight leg of any Gulf-origin cargo, US-bound or not.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 settled $395 (decade high, unchanged); CIF spot ~$365 on squeeze risk under the LME's deep backwardation. The weekend's re-escalation (five Gulf states struck, strait transits near a standstill) strengthens the case for Q4 talks (~Sep) to open at or above the $395 anchor rather than fade toward the lower half of the $340–410 range as this dashboard flagged as possible on Saturday.

European (EDU): last public Platts mark ~$525–560 (03-Jul), unchanged over the weekend. The 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal's outage keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war; the marginal driver — Gulf war-risk freight — has widened again rather than continuing to ease.

US Midwest: SMM DDP ~109.75¢/lb (10-Jul), unchanged. The tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,577 at the 17-Jul cash) is ~two-thirds of the all-in, so US premia track the LME and Washington more than Gulf events directly — but a sustained Gulf disruption raises input-cost and logistics risk for any US-bound metal that transits affected routes. Watch the next weekly print, not the spot survey.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM 08-Jul); Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG flows while Guinea weighs export quotas toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Mining.com/Discovery Alert) — no fresher public print over the weekend.

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia) — no fresher public print over the weekend.

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent) — no fresher public print over the weekend.

alumina_outlook: Alumina: surplus intact on paper — EGA's restart ramps through H2 while SHFE AO sits below its moving averages; ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band holds (FOB Australia ~$338, API ~$303, Platts futures ~$308), all unchanged over the weekend. SMM's June ex-China output dip (−6% y/y) and Guinea's quota push are the counterweights to watch.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments add length. EGA's ramp returns buying to the seaborne market even as Guinea weighs a >$100/dmt export-price push.

Caustic soda / lime / freight: caustic stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia). The delivered-cost leg has moved firmly the wrong way since Friday — Iran's weekend strikes on five Gulf states, including direct damage to Kuwaiti energy infrastructure, and a near-standstill in strait transits mean war-risk cover on Gulf-bound reagent and alumina freight is wider again, not fading. The commodity is still cheap; insuring and moving it through the Gulf is more expensive and less certain than it was even on Friday.

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)

Energy complex firm: Brent ~$85.95 (+2.04% Fri, up >10% on the week on the Iran conflict); EU TTF gas ~€53/MWh — gas-indexed smelter power follows with a lag. Rising carbon-cost exposure.

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.val: Near standstill — war re-escalated over the weekend
logistics.note: The mid-June Islamabad-brokered ceasefire has effectively collapsed since ~08-Jul. Over the weekend Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman), including a direct hit on Kuwaiti energy infrastructure, while the US logged a 7th consecutive night of strikes. Kpler/MarineTraffic data (via NPR) show transits falling to as few as 8 vessels on Thursday 16-Jul, versus a normal ~60/day. ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway.

logistics.name: Southern (Omani) corridor
logistics.val: Not verified reopened; risk has widened, not eased
logistics.note: The workaround corridor discussed in prior weeks has not materialised into confirmed reopening. With Iran now striking Oman itself among the five states hit over the weekend, the corridor's viability is materially more uncertain than it looked as recently as Friday.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: Lloyd's List and the Hormuz Strait Monitor cite war-risk quotes around $10–14m per voyage on some routes, with rates near 2.5% and up to ~5% for US/UK/Israel-linked tonnage — a reversal of Saturday's 'acute spike fading' read. Retain elevated insurance, interdiction and deviation-risk assumptions in all Gulf-routed freight and delivered-cost models until the conflict visibly cools.

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Fell to 2,752 on 17-Jul (−3.10% d/d, third straight down session), down more than 6% on the week — dry-bulk demand softening even as the Gulf war-risk premium (a separate, faster-moving cost line) rebuilds. Price the two legs separately (Trading Economics / Hellenic Shipping News / Baltic Exchange).

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: With strait transits near a standstill and the conflict widening rather than cooling, overland trucking to Red Sea ports is a more actively-used contingency than as of Friday. Keep carrier capacity, costings and lead times current for any Gulf-origin cargo.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.val: Force-majeure & war-risk clauses now firmly live
logistics.note: The weekend's direct strikes on Kuwaiti and other Gulf infrastructure keep force-majeure and deviation clauses firmly live for any Gulf-routed cargo; war-risk surcharge pass-through and interdiction/delay risk are live, worsening contract points to negotiate cargo-by-cargo. Generic supply-chain observation, not company-specific.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (Brent $88.09, up >14% on the week on the weekend's re-escalation — the fastest weekly gain in months; EU TTF gas exposure follows with a lag), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,415.00 at the confirmed 17-Jul official, down 1.40% on the day as the broader complex pulled back Friday).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.

Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China). Secondary feed stays the quiet tightener (UAE scrap export ban, EU tariff changes).

Logistics moved from 'easing' to 'actively worsening' over the weekend: Iran's strikes on five Gulf states (including direct energy-infrastructure damage in Kuwait) and a near-standstill in strait transits mean the delivered-cost and reliability risk for any Gulf-origin cargo — alumina, bauxite, or finished metal — is materially higher heading into the week than this dashboard assumed as of Saturday.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): unchanged count — the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) terminated at $3,855 on 02-Jun-2026 (an extended, war-driven fifth wave with confirmed bearish weekly RSI divergence), and the ABC correction (A $3,359 / B $3,406 / C $3,061.5 on 02-Jul) bottomed inside the $2,950–$3,110 confluence where the .50 retracement overlaps the prior fourth of lesser degree. Base case (≈50%): a new impulse off $3,061.5 — the +$7.50 backwardation and −45.0% YTD stocks support it, and the weekend's sharp Gulf re-escalation (Iran striking five states, ceasefire effectively collapsed) is a fresh bullish catalyst not yet tested by a live session — with a weekly close above $3,260 (.382 trigger) opening $3,400 then $3,620 over the forecast year. Bull (≈30%, raised from 20% given the weekend's escalation): a sustained Gulf disruption drives a direct run at the $3,800 prior high. Bear (≈20%, lowered): a rapid diplomatic circuit-breaker — this conflict has repeatedly flared and cooled since February — unwinds the premium into record Chinese supply, forcing a retest of the $2,950–2,980 zone. Invalidation of the bullish count: a weekly close below $2,950. Confidence moderate; Monday's ring is the first live test of how the weekend's escalation gets priced.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Swing (daily): from the 02-Jul low at $3,061.5 the market built a five-wave micro-impulse into $3,210.5 (wave i), corrected to the .50 retrace at $3,138 (wave ii, tagged and held on a settlement basis), and delivered the provisional wave-iii signature at Tuesday's $3,171 high with the cash–3M spread flipping to backwardation. The drift to the 17-Jul official at $3,146.5 — still the latest published print over the weekend — is a shallow wave-iv-style pullback that has held the $3,138 shelf, with the backwardation steady at +$7.50 through it. Over the weekend Iran struck five Gulf states and the ceasefire effectively collapsed — a fresh bullish catalyst for wave-v that Monday's ring has not yet had the chance to price. Base case (≈50%, raised): iv holds $3,138, then wave-v probes $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382) on the back of the escalation, where a close extends toward $3,330. Bull (≈25%, raised): a gap-and-go straight through $3,260 if Monday's open reflects the weekend's news forcefully. Bear alternate (≈25%, lowered): a rapid de-escalation headline (this conflict has flared and cooled repeatedly) triggers a fade below $3,138 toward $3,085/$3,061.5. Invalidation: daily close below $3,061.5. Confidence moderate — Monday's ring is the tell.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz: war RE-ESCALATED over the weekend — the dominant, worsening driver
premium_drivers.d: Iran struck five Gulf states (Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan, Oman) between Friday night and Sunday morning, including a direct hit on Kuwaiti energy infrastructure; the US logged a 7th consecutive night of strikes. Strait transits fell to as few as 8 vessels Thursday (vs a normal ~60/day). This reverses last week's fading-premium framing — war-risk cover and interdiction risk are rebuilding, not fading.
src: NPR — The U.S. and Iran blow past red lines as they lurch back toward all-out war

premium_drivers.t: The MoU is a much weaker anchor than it looked on Friday
premium_drivers.d: The mid-June Islamabad-brokered ceasefire has effectively collapsed since ~08-Jul, and live wire reporting indicates Iran now considers the US in violation and is suspending commitments under the framework that was meant to reopen the strait by ~18-Aug. A verified corridor reopening remains the fastest premium-unwind trigger; this weekend showed the opposite — a fast re-escalation trigger.
src: ABC News — Iran live updates: Iran says US violated MOU, says its suspending commitments

premium_drivers.t: US Section-232 tariff at 50% — the mechanical leg of US premia
premium_drivers.d: The tariff-$ component of the US Midwest all-in is 50% × LME: ≈$1,577/t at the 17-Jul cash of $3,154 — roughly two-thirds of the ~$2,420 DDP premium. Alcoa's Q2 quantified the other edge: ~$35m more Section-232 cost q/q on its Canadian metal into the US. Unchanged since Thursday.
src: Federal Register — Adjusting Imports of Aluminum and Steel into the United States (50% rate)

premium_drivers.t: EU duty + CBAM + Mozal keep the European floor
premium_drivers.d: The 6% MFN duty and CBAM carbon costs are structural adders under any Rotterdam duty-paid quote, and Mozal (~580 ktpa) remains on care & maintenance — Europe stays structurally tighter than pre-war even as the assessed premium drifts with the war-risk cycle.
src: European Commission — Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t (assessed 06-Jul-2026), via IndexBox

so_what.line: The LME tape is unchanged from Friday (cash $3,154, 3M $3,146.50, cash–3M +$7.50 backwardation) because the exchange is closed over the weekend — but the backdrop it must open against on Monday has deteriorated sharply: Iran struck five Gulf states over the weekend and the ceasefire has effectively collapsed, reversing the 'de-escalation' read this dashboard published Saturday.
points: What's unchanged: the 17-Jul LME official — cash $3,154.00, 3M $3,146.50, cash–3M +$7.50 backwardation, stock 280,100 t (−45.0% YTD) — remains the latest published print because the LME is closed Sat/Sun. Base metals now carry confirmed 17-Jul officials (Cu 3M $13,415, −1.40%; Ni 3M $16,870, −2.82%; Zn 3M $3,529, −1.33%; Pb 3M $1,871, −0.21%). Next ring Mon 20-Jul.

What changed: over the weekend Iran struck FIVE Gulf states — Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, Jordan and Oman — including a direct hit on a Kuwaiti oil-sector site and a desalination plant, in retaliation for renewed US strikes; US CENTCOM logged its 7th consecutive night of strikes on Iran. The mid-June Islamabad-brokered ceasefire, already fraying since ~08-Jul, has effectively collapsed. Strait transits are near a standstill (as few as 8 vessels Thursday vs a normal ~60/day) and Brent jumped to $88.09 (+4.58% Friday, >14% on the week).

Why it matters: this reverses the 'strikes easing, diplomacy advancing' framing this dashboard published Saturday. The cash–3M backwardation has been pricing Gulf tightness for a week on a sub-280kt stock base — it has not yet had the chance to reprice a materially worse escalation, since no LME session has traded since the strikes intensified. Watch whether Monday's ring gaps toward $3,230/$3,260 or whether record Chinese exports (711 kt June) keep a lid on it.

What to watch this week: Monday 20-Jul's LME ring (first repricing opportunity); Norsk Hydro Q2 Wed 22-Jul (07:00 CEST, now a more consequential Gulf-cost read than it looked Friday); whether the ~18-Aug MoU deadline still holds any relevance given reports Iran is suspending commitments under it; and further Gulf war-risk insurance and freight prints as the market catches up to the weekend's news.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
